title: A Dormant Dream Erupts: 18th-Century Mechanical Volcano Built After 250 Years
summary:
In 1775, Sir William Hamilton—British ambassador to Naples and an avid volcanologist—sketched a fantastical contraption: a mechanical device that would recreate the fiery drama of Mount Vesuvius erupting. For 250 years, it remained just an idea on paper. Now, thanks to two engineering students and a trove of modern technology, that dormant dream has roared to life.

Dr. Richard Gillespie, Senior Curator at the University of Melbourne's Faculty of Engineering and Information Technology, unearthed Hamilton's detailed sketch from the Bordeaux Municipal Library. He saw it as the ultimate hands-on challenge. Master's students Xinyu (Jasmine) Xu and Yuji (Andy) Zeng took it on, spending three months in the Creator Space workshop. They swapped Hamilton's envisioned clockwork for laser-cut timber, translucent acrylic, programmable LED strips, and electronic control systems. The result is a mesmerizing tabletop volcano that glows, flickers, and simulates lava flows—all while concealing its inner workings.

"It was a fantastic way to build my hands-on problem-solving skills," Zeng said. "We still faced some of the challenges that Hamilton faced. The light had to be designed and balanced so the mechanisms were hidden from view." The illusion is so effective that viewers see only the eruption, not the engineering.

The device is now the centerpiece of The Grand Tour exhibition at the university's Baillieu Library, open until June 28, 2026. It's a striking piece of science communication, bridging Enlightenment-era curiosity with 21st-century mechatronics. For Xu and Zeng, the project was more than a historical curiosity—it was a crash course in real-world engineering, from 3D printing to troubleshooting electronics. Supervisor Andrew Kogios noted that such experiences "position them well for their future endeavors."

In an age of instant digital gratification, there's something profoundly satisfying about a physical, analog-inspired spectacle. Hamilton's volcano reminds us that the urge to understand and recreate nature's power is timeless—and that sometimes the best way to honor an old idea is to finally build it.
why_it_matters: For students, it's a reminder that engineering isn't just about screens; physical prototyping teaches persistence and creative problem-solving, which are essential for tackling real-world challenges like sustainable design or disaster preparedness.

background_read: Sir William Hamilton (1730–1803) was a British diplomat and passionate volcanologist who served as ambassador to the Kingdom of Naples for 36 years. He published widely on Mount Vesuvius, including detailed illustrations that influenced European understanding of volcanoes.

The sketch of a mechanical Vesuvius, abandoned for 250 years, reflects Enlightenment-era fascination with recreating nature's spectacles. The Bordeaux Municipal Library, where the sketch was rediscovered, is a major repository of historical documents in France.

The exhibit's title, 'The Grand Tour,' refers to the traditional journey young European elites took to Italy to admire classical ruins and natural wonders, including Vesuvius. The students' re-creation uses modern makerspace technology—laser cutting and microcontrollers—showing how today's engineering education blends history with hands-on design.

Hamilton's original design was clockwork-driven, but the students replaced that system with electronic components, demonstrating how far technology has advanced. The result is a physical spectacle in an age dominated by digital screens, reminding us that the urge to understand nature's power through models is timeless.

perspectives.description: The project successfully combines historical curiosity with modern technology, inspiring interdisciplinary learning and giving students hands-on experience with mechatronics and creative problem-solving.

Some might argue that dedicating engineering talent and workshop resources to building a toy volcano is a frivolous use of skills when pressing global problems, such as climate change, need urgent attention.

Historically, mechanical automata and spectacles were used to communicate scientific ideas to the public; this modern version continues that tradition in a new medium, showing how science communication evolves across centuries.

This success could lead to more forgotten historical inventions being rebuilt with modern tools, potentially uncovering lost engineering insights and creating new STEM engagement strategies for museums and schools.
